history: A 45-year-old white male with a history of intractable epilepsy presents to the ED after experiencing a seizure resulting in a fall and forehead laceration. The patient reports approximately three seizures per month but is not currently on any antiepileptic drugs (AEDs). He experienced multiple typical seizures during evaluation, characterized by unresponsiveness, inappropriate laughter, tachycardia, and postictal confusion. Vital signs on arrival: HR 70, RR 18, BP 145/80, SpO2 95%, T 97.3掳F, pain 6/10. Past medical history includes kidney stones and surgery for bilateral clubbed feet. The patient arrived by ambulance and has no known allergies.
outcome: A 45-year-old white male with a history of intractable epilepsy presents to the ED after experiencing a seizure resulting in a fall and forehead laceration. The patient reports approximately three seizures per month but is not currently on any antiepileptic drugs (AEDs). He experienced multiple typical seizures during evaluation, characterized by unresponsiveness, inappropriate laughter, tachycardia, and postictal confusion. Vital signs on arrival: HR 70, RR 18, BP 145/80, SpO2 95%, T 97.3掳F, pain 6/10. Past medical history includes kidney stones and surgery for bilateral clubbed feet. The patient arrived by ambulance and has no known allergies.

history: 53-year-old white male with a history of HCV/EtOH cirrhosis (on Harvoni), COPD with ongoing tobacco use, IDDM, and jugular vein thrombosis (on warfarin), presented to the ED with a chief complaint of chest pain and dyspnea. The patient was referred from a liver clinic due to a 3-day history of intermittent, productive cough with scant yellow sputum and sharp, central chest pain that occurs with coughing. The patient also reported fatigue, mild dyspnea on exertion, and wheezing. There were no symptoms of dyspnea at rest, upper respiratory symptoms, subjective fevers, chills, headache, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, dysuria, rashes, melena, hematochezia, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (PND), or edema. The patient walked into the ED and has allergies to codeine and penicillins. BP 119/71, P 91, RR 22, SpO2 97%, T 98.9掳 F, Pain 8.
outcome: 53-year-old white male with a history of HCV/EtOH cirrhosis (on Harvoni), COPD with ongoing tobacco use, IDDM, and jugular vein thrombosis (on warfarin), presented to the ED with a chief complaint of chest pain and dyspnea. The patient was referred from a liver clinic due to a 3-day history of intermittent, productive cough with scant yellow sputum and sharp, central chest pain that occurs with coughing. The patient also reported fatigue, mild dyspnea on exertion, and wheezing. There were no symptoms of dyspnea at rest, upper respiratory symptoms, subjective fevers, chills, headache, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, dysuria, rashes, melena, hematochezia, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (PND), or edema. The patient walked into the ED and has allergies to codeine and penicillins. BP 119/71, P 91, RR 22, SpO2 97%, T 98.9掳 F, Pain 8.

history: A 76-year-old male with a significant medical history presented to the ED with a chief complaint of left-sided weakness and sensory changes. The patient describes difficulties with lifting his left leg and arm, gripping objects, and feeling clumsy with his left hand. He also reports numbness extending to his mid-calf and mid-forearm. The symptoms developed gradually, with no associated headache, vision changes, speech difficulties, chest pain, or shortness of breath. The patient experienced a fall without headstrike the day before presentation. Allergies include iodine-containing products, sulfa antibiotics, aspirin, and NSAIDs. The patient, who walked into the ED, is currently under neurosurgery service.
outcome: A 76-year-old male with a significant medical history presented to the ED with a chief complaint of left-sided weakness and sensory changes. The symptoms developed gradually, with no associated headache, vision changes, speech difficulties, chest pain, or shortness of breath. The patient experienced a fall without headstrike the day before presentation. Allergies include iodine-containing products, sulfa antibiotics, aspirin, and NSAIDs. The patient, who walked into the ED, is currently under neurosurgery service.

history: 64-year-old white male with a history of type II diabetes mellitus, hypercholesterolemia, depression, anxiety, and chronic neck and low back pain. Recently underwent C5 corpectomy and C6-7 ACDF. He presents with difficulty swallowing, increased swelling around the surgical incision, and hoarseness. Unable to swallow pills or water, regurgitating attempts. Reports no stridor or breathing difficulties. Recently used cocaine, contributing to hypertensive urgency with BP at 180/104, headache, and chest pain. Arrived by ambulance, allergic to Tegretol.
outcome: 64-year-old white male with a history of type II diabetes mellitus, hypercholesterolemia, depression, anxiety, and chronic neck and low back pain. history: A 49-year-old male with a significant medical history including end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis, coronary artery disease, congestive heart failure, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and recent hospitalization for wet gangrene and osteomyelitis, presents with fever and signs of sepsis, including hypotension and tachycardia. The patient also exhibits intermittent agitation and anxiety. He is allergic to dobutamine.
outcome: A 49-year-old male with a significant medical history including end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis, coronary artery disease, congestive heart failure, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and recent hospitalization for wet gangrene and osteomyelitis, presents with fever and signs of sepsis, including hypotension and tachycardia. The patient also exhibits intermittent agitation and anxiety. He is allergic to dobutamine.

history: A 49-year-old African American male presents to the ED with a chief complaint of chest pain. He has a significant medical history, including paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (currently not on anticoagulation), cervical cord compression with associated complications, and a history of substance use (tobacco, alcohol, and IVDU on methadone). The patient describes his chest pain as sub-sternal, radiating to the left arm and jaw, with associated diaphoresis, shortness of breath, and palpitations. The pain occurs at rest, and he reports worsening orthopnea over the past two months. Vital signs are stable, and he has no known drug allergies. The patient walked into the ED and is currently not on anticoagulation despite atrial fibrillation.
outcome: A 49-year-old African American male presents to the ED with a chief complaint of chest pain. The pain occurs at rest, and he reports worsening orthopnea over the past two months. Vital signs are stable, and he has no known drug allergies. The patient walked into the ED and is currently not on anticoagulation despite atrial fibrillation.

history: A 78-year-old white female with a complex medical history including hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypothyroidism, chronic kidney disease stage 3, polymyalgia rheumatica, hyperlipidemia, anemia, hyperparathyroidism, and recent metastatic malignancy status post resection of a cerebellar mass presents to the emergency department with fever, presumed pneumonia, and shock. She arrives via ambulance. The patient has experienced severe hypotension and supraventricular tachycardia (SVT). She was recently intubated for airway protection and had a central line placed for vascular access. Her vital signs on arrival were BP 120/58, HR 150, RR 20, SpO2 97%, and temperature 98.8掳 F, with no reported pain. The patient has a known allergy to penicillins.
outcome: A 78-year-old white female with a complex medical history including hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypothyroidism, chronic kidney disease stage 3, polymyalgia rheumatica, hyperlipidemia, anemia, hyperparathyroidism, and recent metastatic malignancy status post resection of a cerebellar mass presents to the emergency department with fever, presumed pneumonia, and shock. history: A 72-year-old female with a complex medical history including heart failure, aortic stenosis, history of strokes, COPD, and chronic urinary retention with a Foley catheter, presents to the emergency department with shortness of breath, chest pain, and bilateral pedal edema. The patient reports a one-week history of worsening shortness of breath and pedal edema, along with intermittent left-sided chest and arm pain. On the day of admission, she experienced worsening shortness of breath, requiring oxygen supplementation. The patient denies cough, fever, abdominal pain, or recent weight gain. She was transported by ambulance and has no known drug allergies.
outcome: A 72-year-old female with a complex medical history including heart failure, aortic stenosis, history of strokes, COPD, and chronic urinary retention with a Foley catheter, presents to the emergency department with shortness of breath, chest pain, and bilateral pedal edema. history: A 41-year-old white male with a complex medical history including HIV, diabetes mellitus type 2, hidradenitis suppurativa, chronic renal insufficiency, hyperlipidemia, asthma, obstructive sleep apnea, and obesity, presents with a chief complaint of perianal abscess. The patient experiences significant pain exacerbated by sitting, similar to previous abscess episodes, but denies fever, chills, drainage, diarrhea, or bloody stools. He has a history of internal anal condylomata excision and appendectomy. The patient is allergic to erythromycin base. He walked into the ED with vital signs: HR 98, RR 18, BP 126/71, SpO2 100%, Temp 97.8掳F, and pain rated 8.

history: An 80-year-old male with a history of COPD, CAD, CKD, gout, arthritis, and a massive GI bleed presented to the ED with sudden onset of sharp left-sided chest pain. The pain was described as "like a knife," exacerbated by deep breaths, and accompanied by bilateral calf pain. The patient has been undergoing non-operative management for a distal right femur fracture and reports a recent diagnosis of C diff following antibiotics for aspiration pneumonia. He arrived by ambulance and is allergic to aspirin, codeine, ibuprofen, iodinated contrast media, NSAIDs, penicillins, and sulfa antibiotics. Vital signs on arrival were stable, with a heart rate of 82, respiratory rate of 18, blood pressure of 116/62, SpO2 of 95%, and temperature of 98.0掳F, with a reported pain level of 1.

The patient is wheelchair-dependent and has a significant psychiatric history, including passive suicidal ideation and a history of physical and sexual abuse. He arrived at the ED via walk-in and has allergies to penicillins, amitriptyline, and Reglan.

history: A 62-year-old white male with a history of esophageal cancer (status post-esophagectomy), right-sided CVA, chronic pain syndrome on chronic opioids, COPD, and depression presents to the ED with altered mental status. The patient's wife found him having a bizarre conversation, not oriented to date or time, and he had fallen without head trauma. Suspected factors for AMS include possible narcotic overdose (pinpoint pupils, slurred speech). The patient has chronic left-sided weakness post-CVA and was found with a pill bottle, suggesting an extra dose of oxycontin may have been taken. The patient was transported by ambulance and has no known drug allergies. He is alert to person and place but complains of significant thirst.
outcome: A 62-year-old white male with a history of esophageal cancer (status post-esophagectomy), right-sided CVA, chronic pain syndrome on chronic opioids, COPD, and depression presents to the ED with altered mental status. history: 63-year-old white female with a history of ovarian cancer, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, myocardial infarction with cardiac stent, renal insufficiency, right lower extremity DVT/PE on Pradaxa, DM2, GERD, HTN, and a history of seizures related to hypertensive emergency, presents with worsening dyspnea. The patient has a clogged Pleurx catheter initially placed to manage chronic right malignant pleural effusion, which has been producing decreased output. She reports worsened dyspnea post-follow-up visit. Additionally, mild cellulitis is noted around the catheter site. Denies fevers, chills, chest pain, or extremity pain. Arrival: Walk-in. Allergies: hydrocodone, Demerol, Zofran, oxycodone.

history: A 91-year-old male with a significant medical history presented to the ED with the chief complaint of slurred speech and right facial weakness. The patient experienced approximately four episodes of transient slurred speech and right facial droop over the last year, with the most recent episode occurring in the clinic parking lot, lasting about 8 minutes. Symptoms resolved to 95% of baseline by the time of ED arrival. The patient was transported by ambulance. Vital signs were stable upon arrival. Immediate neurological assessment was warranted due to the risk of TIA or stroke.

history: A 67-year-old Black/African American male with a history of hypertension, bilateral pulmonary emboli, stage II chronic kidney disease, and metastatic renal cell carcinoma to the lungs and mediastinum, presented to the ED with abdominal pain, vomiting, nausea, and dyspnea. He recently underwent a right radical nephrectomy one month ago and has been experiencing right lower quadrant pain since the surgery, with non-bilious non-bloody emesis in the past two weeks. This morning, he developed acute right-sided chest pain and dyspnea that resolved with rest. He denies fever but reports taking oxycodone for post-surgical abdominal pain. The patient is a walk-in arrival with no known allergies. Vital signs: BP 166/84, HR 85, RR 16, SpO2 90%, T 99.6掳 F. Pain is rated at 7/10. Family history includes multiple early deaths from coronary artery disease among siblings and renal cancer in the mother.

Past medical history includes TIAs, left basal ganglia infarct with mild residual right weakness, HTN, HLD, depression, GERD, and G6PD deficiency. The patient is a nonsmoker with no alcohol use. Her vital signs were stable upon arrival, and she reported no pain.

She reported falling after the porch railing gave way, landing on her left leg. Her vital signs upon arrival were stable, with a heart rate of 100, respiratory rate of 16, blood pressure of 138/78, SpO2 at 98%, and temperature of 98.1掳F. The patient rated her pain at 2/10.
